channel.title: Ufology on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Ufology on Alien Truth Social

item.title: UFO religion
item.description:
A UFO religion, also known as a UFO cult, is a religion where belief in extraterrestrial entities and communication with them is central. These religions often posit that aliens are interested in humanity’s welfare and can help solve Earth’s problems with their advanced technology and spirituality. Their origins are linked to science fiction, ufology, and UFO sighting narratives. Some historians consider the Aetherius Society or the “I AM” Activity to be the first UFO religions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_religion

item.title: Close encounter
item.description:
In ufology, a “close encounter” is defined as witnessing an unidentified flying object (UFO) at a close range, reducing the likelihood of misidentification. This classification system was first proposed by J. Allen Hynek in his 1972 book, The UFO Experience: A Scientific Inquiry. Hynek’s original scale included six levels, ranging from nocturnal lights to encounters involving animated entities. Later, Jacques Vallée expanded this scale to include close encounters of the fourth and fifth kinds, with the latter referring to human-initiated contact with extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Close_encounter

item.title: Ashtar
item.description:
Ashtar, sometimes called Ashtar Sheran, is the name given to an extraterrestrial being or group of beings that some people claim to have channeled. George Van Tassel was the first to claim receiving an Ashtar message in 1952, and after his introduction, other mediums also claimed contact. These messages often included predictions, such as imminent landings of spaceships on Earth, which ultimately failed to materialize. Ashtar is often depicted as a humanoid with blond hair and European features, and the Ashtar movement is studied as a prominent form of UFO religion.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ashtar_Galactic_Command

item.title: UFO (1956 film)
item.description:
UFO, also known as Unidentified Flying Objects: The True Story of Flying Saucers, is a 1956 semi-documentary that explores the history of the UFO phenomenon in the United States. Through a blend of reenactments and analysis, the film follows the perspective of Air Force press officer Albert M. Chop as he transitions from a skeptic to a believer in the reality of unidentified aircraft. The production is notable for featuring real-life figures and actual footage, such as the famous Mariana UFO incident, and has served as a primary source for subsequent media coverage of the topic.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_%281956_film%29

item.title: Milton William Cooper
item.description:
Milton William “Bill” Cooper (1943–2001) was an American author and radio broadcaster recognized as an influential figure within militia and conspiracy theory circles. In his 1991 book Behold a Pale Horse, Cooper promoted elaborate theories involving the Illuminati, government collusion with extraterrestrials, and the Kennedy assassination. On November 5, 2001, Cooper was fatally shot by Apache County sheriff’s deputies during an armed confrontation at his home in Eagar, Arizona, following years of evading a federal arrest warrant for tax evasion.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milton_William_Cooper

item.title: Linda Moulton Howe
item.description:
Linda Moulton Howe is an American investigative journalist and documentary filmmaker who rose to prominence for her reporting on environmental issues and, later, for her work in the field of ufology. She gained significant attention in 1980 for producing the documentary A Strange Harvest, which explored cattle mutilation theories and alleged extraterrestrial involvement in government activities. Throughout her career, Howe has contributed to numerous television programs and media projects related to UFOs, crop circles, and alien abductions, while frequently appearing as a guest on platforms like Coast to Coast AM. While some supporters regard her as a leading figure in the investigation of unexplained phenomena, critics such as Joe Nickell have characterized her work as credulous and lacking in verified evidence.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linda_Moulton_Howe

item.title: Unidentified flying object
item.description:
An unidentified flying object (UFO), also referred to as an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP), is any aerial object or phenomenon that cannot be immediately identified or explained. While historical reports of unusual aerial sightings date back to ancient times, the term gained cultural prominence following World War II, leading to various government investigations, such as the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book. Although scientific and skeptical organizations, including the Committee for Skeptical Inquiry, consistently find that the majority of these reports are misidentifications of natural phenomena, human technology, or hoaxes, a small percentage remain unexplained. In recent years, entities such as NASA’s UAP independent study team and the Department of Defense’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office have continued to analyze data regarding these encounters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object

item.title: UFO (2018 film)
item.description:
UFO (2018) is a science fiction film written and directed by Ryan Eslinger that follows a mathematics student, played by Alex Sharp, as he attempts to prove the reality of extraterrestrial encounters. Assisted by a professor portrayed by Gillian Anderson, the protagonist decodes complex signals to challenge official government cover-ups regarding a UAP sighting. The film offers a grounded, math-centric take on the UFO genre, though critical reception suggests it serves primarily as a modest nod to classic alien-encounter cinema. Sony Pictures released the project directly to video on September 4, 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_%282018_film%29

item.title: British UFO Research Association
item.description:
The British UFO Research Association (BUFORA) is a UK-based organization founded in 1964 to investigate reported UFO phenomena. The group conducts regular investigations and manages witness support services, reporting that approximately 95% of the cases they review are identified as hoaxes. Throughout its history, the association has faced controversy, including instances where it mistakenly validated fraudulent sightings and endorsed the 1995 film Alien Autopsy as authentic.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_UFO_Research_Association

item.title: Glenn Dennis
item.description:
Glenn Dennis (1925–2015) was a mortician and a founder of the International UFO Museum and Research Center in Roswell, New Mexico. He became a prominent figure in ufology in 1994, claiming to have received classified information regarding the 1947 Roswell UFO incident while working for a local funeral home. However, his credibility faced significant scrutiny after researchers discovered he had provided a fictitious name for a key witness in his account. Despite this, some proponents note that Dennis had shared consistent details about his experiences long before the Roswell case gained widespread public attention.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glenn_Dennis

item.title: Morris K. Jessup
item.description:
Morris K. Jessup (1900–1959) was an American astronomer and ufologist who gained notoriety for his 1955 book, The Case for the UFO. In his writing, Jessup proposed that unidentified flying objects were extraterrestrial in origin and linked them to ancient archaeological sites and prehistoric civilizations. He later became a central figure in the legend of the “Philadelphia Experiment” after receiving correspondence from Carl Meredith Allen, which detailed claims of a secret U.S. Navy naval invisibility project. Jessup died by suicide in Florida on April 20, 1959, following a period of personal instability and declining professional success.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup

item.title: Citizens Against UFO Secrecy
item.description:
Founded in 1977, Citizens Against UFO Secrecy (CAUS) was a non-profit organization that utilized the Freedom of Information Act to advocate for the public release of classified government documents concerning UFOs. Under the leadership of attorney Peter Gersten, the group successfully secured over 900 documents from the CIA in 1979 but faced legal setbacks in subsequent attempts to obtain files from the National Security Agency and state officials. As of 2023, the organization is listed as inactive with its non-profit status revoked.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citizens_Against_UFO_Secrecy

item.title: McMenamins Hotel Oregon UFO Festival
item.description:
The McMenamins UFO Festival is an annual event held in McMinnville, Oregon, that was established in 2000 to commemorate the 1950 McMinnville UFO photographs. It currently stands as the largest UFO-themed event in the state and the second-largest in the United States, attracting up to 10,000 attendees annually. The festival features various guest speakers, such as Luis Elizondo and George Knapp, alongside activities like costume parades and 5K runs. Sponsored by McMenamins, the event has taken place every year since its inception, with the notable exception of 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McMenamins_Hotel_Oregon_UFO_Festival

item.title: Siderella
item.description:
Siderella was a French UFO religion founded in 1966 in Paris by Jean-Paul Appel, which drew on New Age, Buddhist, and Tantric beliefs. The group, known for its frequent name changes and communal lifestyle, centered on the anticipation of an imminent apocalypse where members would be rescued by extraterrestrials. Following allegations of abusive sexual practices and financial exploitation, the organization was formally listed as a cult in a 1995 report by the Parliamentary Commission on Cults in France. Notable members included comic book artists Jean Giraud and Sergio Macedo, the latter of whom collaborated with Appel on the 1982 comic book Timeless Voyage to document the group’s beliefs.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siderella

item.title: Interdimensional UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The interdimensional UFO hypothesis (IUH) proposes that UFO sightings are not necessarily extraterrestrial in origin but result from encounters with entities or phenomena from coexisting, unseen dimensions. Notable ufologists such as Meade Layne, John Keel, J. Allen Hynek, and Jacques Vallée have advanced this theory, suggesting that these anomalies have appeared throughout history under various guises, including mythological or supernatural events. Proponents argue that the recurring patterns and characteristics of these sightings, which often defy conventional space-travel physics, are better explained by the existence of “interlocking universes” rather than planetary visitation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdimensional_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Cryptoterrestrial hypothesis
item.description:
The cryptoterrestrial hypothesis suggests that unidentified aerial phenomena (UFOs) are evidence of an advanced, indigenous civilization hidden on Earth rather than extraterrestrial visitors. The concept draws from late 19th-century literature and mid-20th-century fringe theories, such as the “Shaver Mystery,” to propose that these beings operate from subterranean or hidden environments. While scholars often frame the hypothesis as a thought experiment, the idea remains a recurring theme in modern ufology, appearing in recent discussions by figures like Luis Elizondo. Additionally, the theme of hidden Earth-based civilizations is frequently explored in popular media, including films like The Abyss and Nope, which utilize these narratives to create speculative science-fiction scenarios.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptoterrestrial_hypothesis

item.title: List of ufologists
item.description:
This Wikipedia article provides a categorized list of notable individuals identified as ufologists, defined as people who investigate whether unidentified flying objects are linked to extraterrestrial intelligence. The list is organized by country and features researchers, authors, and public figures involved in UFO studies, such as J. Allen Hynek, Jacques Vallée, and Stanton Friedman. The entry notes that the article relies heavily on primary sources and includes a wide range of individuals spanning from scientific researchers to public advocates and figures associated with various UFO-related claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ufologists

item.title: Majestic 12
item.description:
Majestic 12, also known as Majic-12 or MJ-12, is a purported organization that emerged in fake documents circulated by ufologists in 1984. These documents claim it was a secret committee formed in 1947 by President Harry S. Truman to investigate alien spacecraft. The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) has declared these documents “completely bogus,” and many consider them a hoax. Despite this, the concept remains popular in UFO conspiracy theories and has appeared in various forms of popular culture.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Majestic_12

item.title: The Flying Saucers Are Real
item.description:
Donald Keyhoe’s 1950 book, The Flying Saucers Are Real, investigated United States Air Force reports of UFOs between 1947 and 1950. The book, which followed a similar article published in True magazine, popularized many ideas in ufology. Keyhoe argued that the Air Force had a policy of concealing UFO existence until 1949, after which it shifted to gradual revelation. He also contended that Earth had been visited by extraterrestrials for two centuries, with increased activity after the 1945 atomic bomb test.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Flying_Saucers_Are_Real

item.title: Morris Ketchum Jessup
item.description:
Morris Ketchum Jessup was an American ufologist with a Master of Science degree in astronomy. He is best known for his writings on UFOs, including his 1955 book The Case for the UFO. Jessup’s work explored theories linking ancient monuments to prehistoric superscience and speculated about the nature of UFOs. His death in 1959 was ruled a suicide, but circumstances surrounding it have fueled conspiracy theories, particularly concerning the Philadelphia Experiment.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup

item.title: Kosmopoisk
item.description:
Kosmopoisk, also known as Spacesearch, is a pseudoscientific organization founded in 1980 that investigates ufology, cryptozoology, and other mysteries. The group expanded into an international movement in 2001 and officially registered as the All-Russian Scientific Organization in 2004. Kosmopoisk organizes expeditions to various locations to research anomalous phenomena and creatures. The organization claims to have over 2,500 members in numerous groups across 25 countries and has conducted over 250 expeditions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kosmopoisk

item.title: Secret Space Program and 20 and Back: Fictional Stories as Fact
item.description:
The “secret space program” and “20 and back” are fictional narratives presented as fact within ufology. These stories describe individuals recruited into secret military programs for service on other planets, followed by age regression or time travel to return to their original time. The origins of these stories are linked to a 1970s British spoof documentary called “Alternative 3,” which evolved into a widespread myth. Corey Goode popularized these concepts, particularly on the Gaia program “Cosmic Disclosures,” claiming he was age-regressed after serving as a warrior with alien species. The terms “Secret Space Program” and “20 and Back” are trademarks owned by Goode.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secret_space_program

item.title: National UFO Conference
item.description:
The National UFO Conference (NUFOC) was an annual conference based in San Diego, California, with a mission to present research in ufology. It began in 1964 as the Congress of Scientific Ufologists and was the longest active conference in the United States for presenting information on the UFO phenomenon. In 2004, James W. Moseley retired as Permanent Chairman, and Lisa Davis took over as Executive Director, aiming to increase public awareness of UFO phenomena by expanding contacts within the entertainment community. Davis, active in ufology since 1997, also founded The Foundation For Abductee Research and Support.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_UFO_Conference

item.title: International UFO Congress
item.description:
The International UFO Congress is an annual conference established in 1991 in Arizona that focuses on ufology. It moved to the Phoenix, Arizona area in 2011 after previously being held in Laughlin, Nevada. The event features presentations on topics like alien abduction, UFO sightings, and paranormal experiences, with speakers from around the world. The conference has changed ownership multiple times since its inception.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Congress

item.title: World UFO Day
item.description:
World UFO Day is an awareness day observed annually by some on June 24 and others on July 2. The June date commemorates the first widely reported UFO sighting in the United States, while the July date marks the Roswell incident. The July 2 observance, declared by the World UFO Day Organisation, aims to raise awareness of UFOs and encourage governments to declassify related files. Celebrations involve sky-watching and discussions about the possibility of extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_UFO_Day

item.title: Archives for the Unexplained
item.description:
Archives for the Unexplained (AFU), formerly Archives for UFO research, is a nonprofit foundation in Norrköping, Sweden, dedicated to collecting materials on UFOs, ufology, Forteana, cryptozoology, paranormal phenomena, and folklore. Founded in 1973 as the Work Group for Ufology, it was converted to a charitable foundation in 1980 and moved to its current location. AFU manages approximately 3.5 kilometers of shelf space containing vast collections of books, magazines, newspaper clippings, photographs, and reports, including Scandinavian and British archives. The organization also collects various related items like scale models, paintings, and memorabilia.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archives_for_the_Unexplained

item.title: National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci)
item.description:
The National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci) was a privately financed research organization that operated from 1995 to 2004 in Las Vegas, Nevada. Founded by Robert Bigelow, its purpose was to research fringe science and paranormal topics, particularly ufology, though it also investigated anomalies beyond just UFOs. NIDSci conducted research into areas like cattle mutilation and black triangle reports, and it purchased Skinwalker Ranch. The organization established a hotline in 1999 to receive reports of unusual occurrences, receiving over 5,000 submissions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institute_for_Discovery_Science

item.title: Igor Volke: Estonian Ufologist and Researcher of Environmental Anomalies
item.description:
Igor Volke was an Estonian ufologist and researcher of environmental anomalies, born in 1950 and passing away in 2024. In 1985, he founded AKRAK to collect reports of anomalous environmental phenomena, particularly UFO sightings, across Estonia. This organization was later succeeded by EUFON (Estonian UFO Network). Volke also authored several books on the subject, including “UFO Diaries.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AKRAK

item.title: Dr. Franklin Ruehl: American Actor, Ufologist, and Cryptozoologist
item.description:
Franklin Ruehl was an American actor, ufologist, and cryptozoologist. He hosted the public-access television show Mysteries from Beyond the Other Dominion, which later became the first Sci Fi Channel original series. Ruehl also appeared on various television programs, including Jimmy Kimmel Live! and Ancient Aliens, and hosted his own show, Professor Weird. He claimed to hold a Ph.D. in theoretical nuclear physics from UCLA. Ruehl died in November 2015.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franklin_Ruehl

item.title: UFOetry in 2008, Joshua Poet on right
item.description:
UFOetry is a band from Los Angeles, California, formed in July 2004 by ufologist Joshua Poet. The band performs rock operas centered on UFO phenomena, combining live music and theatrical elements in their multimedia shows. They present their message at UFO festivals and conferences across the continental United States, aiming to spread awareness of UFO phenomena globally. UFOetry has received recognition from the Los Angeles music scene for various achievements, including music video and concert performance awards.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFOetry

item.title: World Contact Day
item.description:
World Contact Day was initiated in March 1953 by the International Flying Saucer Bureau (IFSB) as a day for members to attempt telepathic communication with extraterrestrials. The IFSB theorized that a collective focus on a specific message could transmit it through space if telepathy and alien life were real. The message sent in 1953 invited occupants of interplanetary craft to make a peaceful appearance on Earth. The 1953 celebration inspired the song “Calling Occupants of Interplanetary Craft,” later covered by The Carpenters. In 2013, on its 60th anniversary, World Contact Day was expanded to a full week.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Contact_Day

item.title: Norio Fernando Hayakawa
item.description:
Norio Hayakawa is an American activist born in Yokohama, Japan, in 1944. He is known for his UFOlogy investigations in New Mexico and the American Southwest. Hayakawa has appeared on Coast to Coast AM and was involved in organizing the Area 51 People’s Rally. He also led a Nippon TV crew in 1990 to investigate paranormal activity in Dulce, New Mexico.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norio_Hayakawa

item.title: CEFORA: Argentinean UFO Phenomenon Studies Committee
item.description:
CEFORA, the Argentinean Republic Committee for UFO Phenomena Studies, is an organization formed by various ufology groups in Argentina. Its primary objective is the declassification of all UFO-related phenomena in Argentina. CEFORA has formally requested information from the National Ministry of Defense, the National Presidency, and the Chamber of Deputies. Silvia Pérez Simondini serves as the director of CEFORA and also runs the Visión OVNI UFO Museum in Victoria, Entre Ríos.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cefora
